figures.gloss: The Twelve Olympians returned to open cult — hymned in Greek, honored with incense, libation and barley at reconstructed altars; modern Hellenic theology is emphatically polytheist, while many practitioners also read the gods through the late-antique Platonists, as many powers of one divine order.
figures.facet: A revival with an unbroken philosophical textbook: where other reconstructions work from hostile chronicles, modern Hellenists can hand a newcomer Sallustius's On the Gods and the World — a pagan catechism written by pagans — which shapes the one-through-many strand in the movement's self-understanding.
figures.provenance: YSEE's published theology and ritual texts; Sallustius, On the Gods and the World (4th c. CE) as a common modern catechism; scholarly treatment in Kathryn Rountree (ed.), Contemporary Pagan and Native Faith Movements in Europe (2015).

figures.gloss: The movement's flagship public festival, held each summer since 1996 in the foothills of Mount Olympus at Litochoro — days of procession, athletic games, theatre and open ritual under the mountain of the gods, named for Prometheus the fire-giver.
figures.facet: The choice of patron is the program: by naming its great festival for the Titan who defied heaven for humankind's sake, the revival casts itself as bearer of stolen light against an establishment it regards as the usurper — self-definition performed as festival calendar.
figures.provenance: The Prometheia's history is documented in Greek press coverage and in Rountree 2015; the festival is organized by the Hellenic ethnic-religion movement around YSEE and allied groups.

figures.gloss: The rebuilt domestic core of the religion: lustral water (khernips) before prayer, daily offerings at a household shrine, the hearth honored as Hestia's — first and last of libations — and the storeroom jar of Zeus Ktesios, all reassembled from classical literary and epigraphic sources.
figures.facet: Reconstruction at its most granular and most defensible: unlike the great civic cults, the household layer is recoverable from surviving texts in fine detail, so the revival's strongest continuity claim runs through the kitchen, not the temple.
figures.provenance: Reconstructed from classical sources (Hesiod's Works and Days; the Zeus Ktesios and Hestia testimonia gathered in modern scholarship such as Walter Burkert's Greek Religion, 1985); codified for practice in YSEE and practitioner handbooks.

figures.gloss: Writer and organizer (1959–2019), co-founder of YSEE in 1997 and its long-time public face; his book Demolish Them (1994), a chronicle of the late-antique destruction of Hellenic temples and cult, gave the movement its historical indictment of the Orthodox establishment it still contends with.
figures.facet: The founder as prosecutor: Rassias built the revival's identity less on romantic continuity than on documented grievance — the argument that Hellenic religion did not fade but was suppressed, which keeps the movement's tension with the Church of Greece structural rather than incidental.
figures.provenance: Vlassis Rassias, Es Edafos Ferein ('Demolish Them', 1994); YSEE organizational history; obituaries and coverage of his death in 2019; Rountree 2015.

figures.gloss: A set of ancient aphorisms — 'Know thyself,' 'Nothing in excess,' 'Control anger,' among some 147 recorded on inscriptions such as the 4th-century BCE stele at Ai Khanoum in Afghanistan — inscribed in antiquity at Delphi and attributed to the Seven Sages; adopted whole by YSEE and practitioner literature as the revival's ready-made ethical code.
figures.facet: The movement's ethics arrive pre-assembled rather than reconstructed: unlike ritual practice, which must be rebuilt piece by piece from scattered testimonia, the Maxims give modern Hellenism a complete, ancient, and already-portable moral catechism requiring no modern authorship at all.
figures.provenance: The Ai Khanoum stele (excavated 1966, Afghanistan, an actual ancient inscribed copy of the Maxims); the maxims' transmission and attribution to the Seven Sages discussed in Joseph Fontenrose and later classical scholarship; adopted in YSEE's published ethical literature.

figures.gloss: The cultivated relation of gift-and-return between worshipper and god: an offering given well and consistently builds a store of goodwill (kharis) that disposes the god to answer prayer and favor the giver — the stated operative theology behind the household libations and khernips already practiced in the revival.
figures.facet: Reciprocity is named as a mechanism, not left implicit: modern Hellenist practitioner writing is unusually explicit that offerings are not payment or supplication but the deliberate accumulation of a relationship, giving the tradition's whole ritual layer a single, stated theological rationale.
figures.provenance: Ancient usage discussed in Walter Burkert, 'Greek Religion' (1985), on the reciprocal logic of sacrifice; modern articulation in YSEE and practitioner handbooks (e.g. Hellenion's public ritual guides) naming kharis as the mechanism of offering.

figures.gloss: Roman emperor (r. 361-363 CE), raised Christian but a secret devotee of the old gods, who on taking sole power announced the empire's return to Hellenic cult, reopened temples, restored sacrifice, and wrote his own philosophical defenses of polytheism before dying on campaign against Persia after barely 20 months' reign. Honored across the modern movement as the last emperor of the old religion.
figures.facet: He supplies the revival's proof that suppression, not natural decline, ended the old cult: because an emperor of the empire itself chose to reverse Christianization and nearly succeeded, modern Hellenism can point to Julian as evidence its religion was a live, restorable tradition cut short by force rather than one that simply died of age.
figures.provenance: Julian's own surviving works (Against the Galilaeans, Hymn to King Helios); Ammianus Marcellinus's contemporary history; modern treatment in G.W. Bowersock, 'Julian the Apostate' (1978); veneration as a movement symbol documented in Rountree (ed.), Contemporary Pagan and Native Faith Movements in Europe (2015).

figures.gloss: The reconstructed opening observance of each lunar month: household offerings to Zeus Ktesios, Apollo Noumenios, Hestia, and the ancestral gods on the day of the first visible crescent, following the Attic civil calendar's lunar reckoning attested in classical sources.
figures.facet: Time itself is re-consecrated monthly rather than only annually: where the Prometheia is a single yearly public festival, Noumenia rebuilds the ordinary recurring texture of ancient religious time at household scale, giving the revival a devotional rhythm as fine-grained as its ancient model.
figures.provenance: Attic calendrical evidence gathered in H.W. Parke, 'Festivals of the Athenians' (1977); Hesiod's Works and Days on lucky and unlucky days of the month; reconstructed for practice in YSEE and Hellenion practitioner calendars.

edges.note: The defining revival-of seam: the modern Dodekatheon deliberately restores the classical Olympian cult, Zeus at its head, worked from the ancient sources in the gods' own language — a documented reach across a fifteen-century break.
The movement's chief festival is explicitly named for and dedicated to Prometheus — the ancient fire-thief adopted as the emblem of a religion reclaiming what it holds was taken from it.

Class seam of European reconstructionism: offering rites rebuilt from period sources on the reciprocal gift-logic — libation and khernips beside mead and horn — parallel modern reconstructions, not contact.

Intra-tradition: the revival rebuilds ancient religion's two ancient halves together — the public festival under Olympus and the daily cult of hearth and storeroom, polis-scale and household-scale of one practice.

Both modern revivals ground their theology in a single core inherited text read as primary lore rather than revealed scripture — the Codex Regius's Christian-era compilation of pre-Christian poems beside Sallustius's pagan-authored catechism — though the two texts differ sharply in origin, one folklore transcription, the other philosophical treatise.

Class seam of 20th-century polytheist reconstruction: two founding organizers, a generation apart, who each built the durable institutional vehicle (Ásatrúarfélagið 1972; YSEE 1997) that carried a suppressed pre-Christian religion into state-recognized modern practice.
Intra-tradition: Noumenia is the household cult's own monthly opening rite — the same khernips-and-hearth practice performed on a fixed lunar schedule rather than daily.
Intra-tradition: kharis is the stated theological mechanism the household offerings are explicitly built to cultivate — the concept names what the rite does.

Intra-tradition: the revival pairs a restored theology (the Twelve) with a ready-made ancient ethics (the Maxims) as its two foundational textual pillars, belief and conduct sourced separately but held together in practice.

Intra-tradition: Julian supplies the ancient proof-figure and Rassias the modern chronicler of the same grievance narrative — the old religion suppressed by force rather than allowed to fade, one embodying the attempted reversal, the other its documented history.

Class seam of modern reconstructionism: both adopt a short numbered list of ethical maxims as a portable moral code for a revived polytheism — the Delphic Maxims genuinely ancient and inscribed, the Nine Noble Virtues a 1970s codification from saga material — a shared move, different degrees of ancient authorship.

Class seam of European reconstructionism: both name the same reciprocal gift-logic as their offerings' explicit theological mechanism — kharis built through libation, the blót's 'do ut des' built through mead and food — parallel modern articulations of an ancient reciprocity idea.

dossier.essence: Modern Hellenism (Hellenic ethnic religion) is the organized revival of ancient Greek polytheism in Greece and its diaspora, its formative decade the 1990s: the first Prometheia festival was held below Mount Olympus in 1996 and the Supreme Council of Ethnic Hellenes (YSEE) was founded in Athens in 1997. The Greek state recognized 'Hellenic Ethnic Religion' as a known religion in 2017. Its central claim, pressed by co-founder Vlassis Rassias's chronicle of late-antique temple destruction, is that Hellenic religion did not fade but was suppressed — and can therefore be resumed, not merely reinvented.
dossier.cosmos: The theology is emphatically polytheist — the Twelve Olympians restored to open cult — but many practitioners read the gods through the late-antique Platonists, as many ungenerated powers of one eternal, divinely ordered cosmos; Sallustius's fourth-century treatise 'On the Gods and the World', a pagan catechism written by pagans, circulates as a common modern textbook. Afterlife views are held loosely and vary with the ancient sources a practitioner leans on, from the Homeric shade-world to Platonic and Orphic ideas of the soul's ascent; the movement stakes its identity on cult and virtue rather than on a fixed eschatology.
dossier.practice: The rebuilt core is the household: lustral water (khernips) before prayer, daily offerings at a domestic shrine, Hestia honored first and last at the hearth — the layer of ancient religion most finely recoverable from surviving texts. Publicly, YSEE congregations keep a festival calendar of processions, hymns in Greek, libations and barley offerings, with the summer Prometheia at Litochoro as the movement's flagship gathering; ethics center on the classical virtues. Tension with the Church of Greece is structural, since the movement defines itself as the suppressed predecessor of the establishment around it.
dossier.canon: There is no closed scripture: the ancient corpus itself — Homer, Hesiod, the Homeric and Orphic hymns, and philosophical texts like Sallustius — serves as both source and liturgy, codified for practice in YSEE and practitioner handbooks.
dossier.reflects: The reconstruction that never lost its books — a revival able to work from an unbroken high-literary canon written by its own ancient practitioners, which lets it frame itself as suppressed rather than extinct.
sources: Kathryn Rountree (ed.), 'Contemporary Pagan and Native Faith Movements in Europe: Colonialist and Nationalist Impulses' (2015)
Sallustius, 'On the Gods and the World', trans. Thomas Taylor (1793)

links.cite: Sallustius, 'On the Gods and the World', trans. Thomas Taylor (1793)
Kathryn Rountree (ed.), 'Contemporary Pagan and Native Faith Movements in Europe: Colonialist and Nationalist Impulses' (2015)
